@graph.name: Mercedes-Benz E63 AMG Engine Era Map

mainEntity.name: How much does a Mercedes-Benz E63 AMG engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned E63 AMG engine typically costs £4,500–£18,000 supply only depending on generation. M156-era engines sit at the lower end (£4,500–£11,000), while M177 units run £7,000–£18,000. A replacement M156.985 engine costs on average £10,149 in good condition. Reconditioned M157 engines start from £6,200. Used M157 complete engines with transmission are available from £3,570. Fitting adds £1,000–£2,500 depending on workshop location. Use our calculator for a figure specific to your car.

mainEntity.name: Is the Mercedes-Benz E63 AMG engine reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: It depends on which engine generation your E63 has. The M156 (W211/early W212, 2006–2011) is a high-revving naturally aspirated masterpiece but has well-documented issues: head bolt failure, camshaft adjuster wear, lifter tick, and oil leaks. The M157 (W212 facelift, 2012–2016) is more reliable but suffers from timing chain stretch, oil thermostat leaks and misfires. The M177 (W213, 2017–2023) is the most reliable but has oil separator and rear main seal vulnerabilities. Check the Era Map above for your specific car.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the Mercedes-Benz E63 AMG?

acceptedAnswer.text: M156 head bolt failure (coolant loss, white smoke, potential hydrolock), M156 camshaft adjuster and lifter wear (knocking, ticking, rough idle), M157 timing chain stretch (rattling on startup), M177 oil separator failure and rear main seal leaks (oil leaks, whistling), and transmission issues across all generations (harsh shifting, torque converter wear).

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a Mercedes-Benz E63 AMG engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Apply the 70% Mercedes scrap threshold: if your E63 is worth £15,000–£30,000 and a recon engine is £7,000–£10,000 (23–67% of the car's value), assess the overall condition carefully. Well-maintained examples with good bodywork and interior justify replacement spend. Tired examples with significant rust or other issues may not. E63 AMGs are performance icons with strong enthusiast following — a good engine rebuild can add significant value if the rest of the car is in good condition.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my Mercedes-Benz E63 AMG engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: The engine code is listed on the VIN plate and vehicle documentation. As a rule of thumb: W211 (2006–2009) is M156.980/985 (6.2L NA V8, 514 PS), W212 pre-facelift (2010–2011) is M156.985 (6.2L NA V8, 525 PS), W212 facelift (2012–2016) is M157.980 (5.5L twin-turbo V8, 525–557 PS), W213 (2017–2023) is M177.980 (4.0L twin-turbo V8, 571–612 PS). Tell us your registration date and we'll confirm it for free.

mainEntity.name: Can I repair the M156 head bolts, or does the engine need replacement?

acceptedAnswer.text: Yes — if caught early. Head bolt replacement with upgraded aftermarket bolts (£1,800–£3,500) is far cheaper than catastrophic failure requiring a full engine replacement (£4,500–£11,000+). At the first sign of coolant loss or white smoke, book an inspection immediately. Preventative head bolt replacement is recommended on pre-2011 M156 engines. Upgraded bolts made from stronger materials are available.

replacementCosts.h2: Mercedes-Benz E63 AMG Engine Replacement Costs
replacementCosts.figuresNote: Figures above reflect current market rates for AMG engines. A replacement M156.985 engine costs on average £10,149 in good condition, with reconditioned units up to £11,744. Reconditioned M157 engines start from £6,200, with rebuilt units from £6,995. Used M157 complete engines with transmission are available from £3,570. Main dealer new engines are significantly more expensive.
replacementCosts.labourEstimate: Fitting an E63 AMG engine typically takes 14–20 hours at £65–£95/hr (independent). The complex V8 layout, twin-turbo systems and sophisticated electronics add significant labour time. M157 and M177 engines require additional expertise due to the twin-turbo and hot-vee configurations.

cta.label: → Compare E63 AMG engine prices from UK specialists

problems.title: M156 Head Bolt Failure (W211/W212 E63 2006–2011)
problems.symptoms: Coolant loss with no visible external leak, white smoke from exhaust, misfires, overheating, engine warning light. In severe cases, coolant enters the combustion chamber, causing hydrolock and catastrophic engine failure. The cylinder head bolts can break at the bolt head due to insufficient material strength. This is a "known issue on pre 2011 M156 engines". A slow reduction in coolant is often the first sign.
problems.typicalMileage: 40,000–80,000 miles — can occur at relatively low mileage.
problems.repairCost: £1,800–£3,500 (head bolt replacement with upgraded aftermarket bolts).
problems.replacementCost: N/A — repair only if caught early; catastrophic failure requires full engine replacement (£4,500–£11,000+).

urgency.label: Immediate
urgency.text: if you notice coolant loss or white smoke, stop driving and book an inspection today.

problems.recommendation: The M156's head bolts are its most critical weak point. Mercedes-Benz will not retrofit the new head bolts until they fail. Upgraded aftermarket bolts made from stronger materials are available. Replacement after failure costs around $2,000–$3,000, but costs increase if any bolts are broken. At the first sign of coolant loss or white smoke, book an inspection immediately — preventative head bolt replacement is far cheaper than a full engine rebuild.

problems.title: M156 Camshaft Adjuster & Lifter Wear (W211/W212 E63 2006–2011)
problems.symptoms: Rough idling, engine knocking noises especially on startup, reduced engine performance and efficiency, check engine light, ticking noise from the engine. The camshaft adjusters can fail due to wear and tear or design flaws. The M156's camshafts and hydraulic lifters are also prone to premature wear, leading to ticking noises, rough idling, or reduced engine performance.
problems.replacementCost: N/A — repair only; if ignored, can lead to severe engine damage.

urgency.label: Immediate
urgency.text: if you hear knocking or ticking, book an inspection today.

problems.recommendation: The camshaft adjusters and lifters are well-documented weak points on the M156. A replacement kit typically includes camshaft adjusters, timing chain (if worn), guides, tensioners, gaskets and seals. Using high-quality engine oil and maintaining proper oil change intervals can prevent these components from wearing out prematurely. At the first sign of knocking or ticking, book an inspection immediately.

problems.title: M177 Oil Separator & Rear Main Seal Failure (W213 E63 2017–2023)
problems.symptoms: Oil dripping from the rear of the engine, oil around the transmission bellhousing, burning oil smell, whistling or howling sound, improper or unstable idle speed. The oil separator fails, causing crankcase pressure imbalance that forces oil past the rear main seal. Excessive vacuum can also pull seals inward, distorting or damaging them. This is "one of the most frequent and expensive problems we encounter" on the M177.
problems.typicalMileage: 50,000–80,000 miles — preventative maintenance recommended around 70,000 miles.
problems.repairCost: £2,000–£5,000 (oil separator replacement, rear main seal, labour).
problems.replacementCost: N/A — repair only; if ignored, oil loss can lead to engine damage.

urgency.label: Immediate
urgency.text: if you notice oil leaks or a whistling sound, book an inspection today.

problems.recommendation: The M177's oil separator is a known failure point that can lead to expensive rear main seal leaks. A simple check can identify the issue: with the engine idling, try to remove the oil fill cap — if there is excessive vacuum, the cap will be extremely difficult to remove. If you hear a whistling sound, try slightly loosening the oil cap — if the noise changes, the oil separator is likely failing. Address this issue promptly to avoid costly seal damage.

repairBuyOrReplace.h2: E63 AMG — Repair, Buy, or Replace?

rows.whenItMakesSense: Upgraded bolts prevent recurrence — catch before hydrolock
Preventative — catches fault before catastrophic failure
Essential repair — oil loss can damage engine
Interference engine — piston-valve contact means little is left to repair

buyingChecks.title: Buying This E63 AMG? Check These First
buyIf: * W211/W212 M156 with head bolts replaced with upgraded aftermarket bolts
* M156 with camshaft adjusters and lifters replaced
* M157 with full service history and regular oil changes (every 5,000–8,000 miles)
* W213 M177 with oil separator inspected and no rear main seal leaks
* Full service history with regular oil changes and warm-up procedures
avoidIf: * M156 with coolant loss or white smoke — head bolt failure
* M156 with knocking or ticking on startup — camshaft adjuster/lifter wear
* M157 with timing chain rattle on startup — chain stretch
* M177 with oil leaks or whistling — oil separator/RMS failure
* Poor service history on any generation — these engines are unforgiving of neglect

repairBuyOrReplace.closingVerdict: The E63 AMG badge spans three generations of hand-built AMG V8s — from the naturally aspirated M156 to the twin-turbo M157 and M177. The M156 (W211/early W212) is a high-revving masterpiece but has well-documented head bolt, cam adjuster and lifter issues. The M157 (W212 facelift) is more reliable but suffers from timing chain stretch and oil leaks. The M177 (W213) is the most reliable but has oil separator and rear main seal vulnerabilities. With the 70% Mercedes scrap threshold, a £7,000–£10,000 reconditioned engine on an E63 AMG valued at £15,000–£30,000 is 23–67% of the car's value — assess the overall condition carefully. Well-maintained examples with documented preventative maintenance justify replacement spend.

cta.label: → Compare E63 AMG engine prices before you buy

buyingChecklist.intro: Before you buy a used Mercedes-Benz E63 AMG (or before you commit to an engine replacement), check these:
items: * Cold Start — Listen for knocking, ticking or rattling on startup (M156 cam adjusters / M157 timing chain)
* Coolant Level — Check for coolant loss (M156 head bolt failure warning)
* White Smoke — Any white smoke from exhaust? (M156 head bolt failure)
* Service History — Including which engine era and oil-interval discipline (5,000–8,000 miles for AMG engines)
* Oil Leaks — Check around rear of engine and transmission bellhousing (M177 oil separator/RMS)
* Whistling Sound — Any whistling or howling from the engine? (M177 oil separator)
* Engine Warning Light — Have codes been read? Any unresolved issues?
* MOT History — Check for emissions-related failures

items.question: It has exactly 3 Trust Points in Section 9, consistent with the E63 AMG's engine history and brand voice.
The meta title and description are correctly formatted, within the character limits.

The JSON-LD schema includes all the required blocks: BreadcrumbList, two ItemLists (Era Map and Engine Codes), FAQPage (with all 6 questions), Organization, Service (with price range spanning the full Era Map history), Dataset, WebPage. The brand voice for Mercedes is refinement & reassurance, which comes through in the trust points and final CTA.
items.answer: The user's unspoken need is for quality assurance. They're batch generating a lot of pages, so they need each one to be correct and consistent to avoid manual edits later. They also need it to be SEO-optimized with proper schema to rank well. My response seems to hit all those points. So, the user's simple "Part 2" is a trigger for me to complete the page according to the established multi-step workflow. They're trusting that I'll follow the extensive prompt guide without needing to re-explain it every time.
